aggregate_governed_rationale: Binary classification; per-item baselines are necessarily 0%/100%; powerability governed by aggregate baseline + paired McNemar (which ignores concordant both-correct ceiling pairs, so ceiling items cannot inflate positive lift; floor items are the discriminative set); aggregate baseline 92% = CEILING/no-power
source: Combined from 7 source files. authored: first-principles-constraint, reversibility-doors, second-order-consequence, cynefin-classify; harder-conceptual items from evals/datasets/calibrated/conceptual-reframes-harder-pool.jsonl (generated by evals/datasets/calibrated/build-harder-conceptual-systems-pools.js, m3-harder-data); external: debias-authored, glm-distractor
leakage:
First-principles-constraint, reversibility-doors, second-order-consequence, and harder-conceptual are locally authored datasets — zero external leakage. Debias-authored items are locally authored cognitive bias scenarios with anchoring bias types. Some items may resemble classic cognitive bias examples from psychology literature which models may have encountered. GLM distractor items may have training data overlap. Overall leakage risk is low because locally authored items dominate the target set.

Source breakdown: 1 external dataset(s) with potential training-data leakage; 5 authored dataset(s) with zero external leakage;

label.definition: Binary decision quality on conceptual reframing tasks: whether the model correctly identifies whether a constraint is fundamental (true) or merely conventional (false); whether a decision is a one-way door (true) or two-way door (false); whether a proposed action's second-order effects undermine its stated goal (false) or the goal is achieved (true). Harder items (m3-harder-data) target subtle distinctions: constraints that look fundamental but are conventional, doors with hidden irreversibility, and second-order effects with complex interactions. Off-target lookalikes included for discrimination.

calibration.exemption: CEILING-NEEDS-HARDER-DATA. After sourcing 25 genuinely harder items (subtle first-principles constraints where fundamental-vs-conventional is non-obvious, nuanced reversibility doors, and complex second-order scenarios), claude-sonnet-4-6 still achieves ~92% aggregate baseline accuracy on placebo/baseline condition (25 labeled target items at K_TRIALS=5). Only 1 item (fp-harder-01, PCI-DSS constraint) landed in-band at 60%. All conventional-constraint, reversibility, and second-order items remained ceiling. The ~8pp headroom leaves insufficient room for the target ≥5pp lift with statistical power. The solver demonstrates near-perfect pattern recognition for constraint-classification and door-classification tasks even on intentionally ambiguous cases. This family requires genuinely HARDER items — potentially multi-step reasoning tasks rather than binary classification — to reach the [0.40,0.70] band. Per Invariant #4, this is NOT a kill verdict. Per library/harness.md aggregate rule: aggregate baseline ~92% = genuine NO-POWER ceiling (only ~8pp headroom).
calibration.exemption_reason: CEILING-NEEDS-HARDER-DATA: 92% baseline after good-faith harder-item sourcing effort. 1 of 25 items in-band. Conventional/reversibility/second-order items remain trivially easy for claude-sonnet-4-6.
calibration.harder_items_note: The m3-harder-data pool added 25 target items with subtle first-principles distinctions (constraints that look dismissible but are fundamental, constraints that look fundamental but are conventional), nuanced reversibility scenarios (technically reversible but practically one-way, and vice versa), and complex second-order effects. Batch 1 (first-principles fundamental items) showed partial headroom (68%, 1 in-band), but conventional-constraint identification, reversibility classification, and second-order reasoning remained at 100% ceiling. The solver's pattern-matching ability for 'policy language' vs 'mathematical/legal language' makes these binary distinctions trivial.

previous_calibration.note: Original calibration on easier items — 100% ceiling. Superseded by harder-pool calibration above.

item_class_definitions.target: Items that directly measure the family value surface — in-domain, skill-relevant items where the skill is expected to help.
item_class_definitions.near_miss: Items that are similar to the family domain but not an exact match. Useful for calibration but may not benefit from the skill.
item_class_definitions.distractor: Items that are clearly off-target (target=false). Used to measure false-positive rate and specificity.
item_class_definitions.ambiguous: Items whose relevance to the family is unclear. Excluded from decisive splits.
